title: Prevention and Early Detection of Cancer
authors: Jennifer M. Croswell, Otis W. Brawley, Barnett S. Kramer

items.title: Why smoking cessation saves more lives from cancer than any other public-health activity
items.content:
Tobacco is the dominant modifiable cancer risk factor.
Smokers have an ~1 in 3 lifetime risk of dying prematurely from a tobacco-related cancer, cardiovascular, or pulmonary disease, and cigarette smoking shortens life expectancy on average by a decade.
Those who stop smoking have a 30–50% lower 10-year lung cancer mortality rate compared with those who continue, despite some carcinogen-induced gene mutations persisting for years.
Smoking cessation and avoidance would save more lives from cancer than any other public health activity.
keyPoints: Smokers: ~1 in 3 lifetime risk of premature tobacco-related death; ~decade shorter life
Quitting → 30–50% lower 10-year lung cancer mortality
Tobacco causes more deaths from cardiovascular disease than from cancer
items.reference: SMOKING CESSATION: "Smoking cessation and avoidance would save more lives from cancer than any other public health activity."

items.title: Why tobacco-use prevention is fundamentally a pediatric issue
items.content:
Most lifelong smokers are recruited in adolescence.
More than 80% of adult American smokers began smoking before the age of 18 years, making tobacco-use prevention a pediatric issue.
While traditional cigarette smoking has declined substantially among high-school students, electronic-cigarette use rose rapidly (about 20% of high-school students were current users in 2020).
Providers should query all patients on tobacco use and offer smokers assistance in quitting or referrals — even simple clinician advice can be of benefit.
keyPoints: >80% of adult smokers started before age 18 → prevention is pediatric
Cigarette use is falling but e-cigarette use rose sharply (~20% of high-schoolers in 2020)
Ask every patient about tobacco; brief advice and referral help
items.reference: SMOKING CESSATION: "More than 80% of adult American smokers began smoking before the age of 18 years."

items.title: How to support a smoker who is trying to quit
items.content:
Quitting is a staged behavioral process aided by pharmacology.
The smoker who is quitting passes through identifiable stages — contemplation, an action phase, and a maintenance phase — and smokers who quit completely are more likely to succeed than those who gradually cut down or switch to lower-tar/nicotine cigarettes.
Heavy smokers may need an intensive program combining counseling, behavioral strategies, and pharmacologic adjuncts: nicotine replacement (gum, patches, sprays, lozenges, inhalers), bupropion, and/or varenicline.
Light/low-tar cigarettes are not safer because smokers inhale them more frequently and deeply.
keyPoints: Quitting completely beats gradual reduction or switching to 'lighter' cigarettes
Combine counseling/behavioral strategies with NRT, bupropion, and/or varenicline
'Light/low-tar' cigarettes are not safer (compensatory deeper inhalation)
items.reference: SMOKING CESSATION: "Smokers who quit completely are more likely to be successful than those who gradually reduce the number of cigarettes smoked or change to lower-tar or lower-nicotine cigarettes."

items.title: Why dietary fat reduction has not been proven to prevent cancer
items.content:
Observational diet-cancer associations have repeatedly failed to hold up in trials.
International ecologic studies suggest high-fat diets are associated with breast, colon, prostate, and endometrial cancers, but dietary fat has not been proven to cause cancer and case-control/cohort studies conflict.
The Women's Health Initiative randomized >100,000 women and found that a low-fat, high-fruit/vegetable/fiber diet did not reduce invasive breast cancer over 8 years, nor reduce colorectal cancer incidence.
Evidence does not currently establish the anticarcinogenic value of vitamin, mineral, or nutritional supplements beyond a balanced diet.
keyPoints: High-fat diet associations are not proven causal; trials are negative
WHI dietary intervention did not reduce breast or colorectal cancer
Supplements above a balanced diet have no established anticarcinogenic value
items.reference: DIET MODIFICATION: "although dietary fat intake was lower in the diet intervention group, invasive breast cancers were not reduced over an 8-year follow-up period compared to the control group."

items.title: How body mass index relates to cancer risk
items.content:
Excess adiposity modestly raises the risk of several specific cancers.
Risk of certain cancers increases modestly (relative risks generally 1.0–2.0) as BMI rises beyond 25 kg/m².
A cohort of >5 million adults found each 5 kg/m² rise in BMI linearly associated with cancers of the uterus, gallbladder, kidney, cervix, thyroid, and leukemia.
High BMI appears to have an inverse association with prostate and premenopausal breast cancer.
keyPoints: BMI >25 modestly increases risk (RR ~1.0–2.0) of several cancers
Each 5 kg/m² linked to uterus, gallbladder, kidney, cervix, thyroid cancers and leukemia
Inverse association with prostate and premenopausal breast cancer
items.reference: ENERGY BALANCE: "each 5 kg/m² increase in BMI was linearly associated with cancers of the uterus, gallbladder, kidney, cervix, thyroid, and leukemia."

items.title: Why sunscreen reduces actinic keratoses but may not prevent melanoma
items.content:
UV protection has different effects on different skin cancers.
Nonmelanoma skin cancers (basal and squamous cell) are induced by cumulative UV exposure, whereas the evidence for a direct association between cumulative UV exposure and melanoma is weaker.
Sunscreens decrease the risk of actinic keratoses (the precursor to squamous cell skin cancer), but melanoma risk may not be reduced — sunscreens prevent burning but may encourage prolonged sun exposure and may not filter the wavelengths that cause melanoma.
Notably, about 70–80% of melanomas arise de novo rather than from existing benign nevi.
keyPoints: Cumulative UV → nonmelanoma skin cancer; melanoma link is weaker
Sunscreen reduces actinic keratoses but may not reduce melanoma (and may prolong exposure)
~70–80% of melanomas arise de novo, not from pre-existing nevi
items.reference: SUN AVOIDANCE: "Sunscreens decrease the risk of actinic keratoses, the precursor to squamous cell skin cancer, but melanoma risk may not be reduced."

items.title: How initiators and promoters drive carcinogenesis
items.content:
Carcinogenesis is a multistep process with distinct intervention points.
Initiation is the initial genetic/epigenetic change (inherited or acquired via physical, infectious, or chemical carcinogens), while promoters cause the initiated cell and its microenvironment to progress and change phenotypically.
Promoters include hormones such as androgens (linked to prostate cancer) and estrogen (linked to breast and endometrial cancer), and some components of cigarette smoke are 'complete carcinogens' acting as both initiators and promoters.
Chemoprevention uses natural or synthetic agents — antimutagenic, hormone-modulating, anti-inflammatory, antiproliferative, or proapoptotic — to reverse, suppress, or prevent carcinogenesis before invasive malignancy.
keyPoints: Initiation = the genetic/epigenetic change; promotion = progression of the initiated cell
Promoters include androgens (prostate) and estrogen (breast/endometrium)
Some cigarette-smoke components are 'complete carcinogens' (initiator + promoter)
items.reference: CANCER CHEMOPREVENTION: "Promoters include hormones such as androgens, linked to prostate cancer, and estrogen, linked to breast and endometrial cancer."

items.title: Why ATBC and CARET prove chemoprevention must be tested in randomized trials
items.content:
Plausible, observation-backed supplements can prove harmful when finally tested.
In the ATBC trial of male smokers, lung cancer incidence and mortality were significantly INCREASED in those receiving β-carotene, and α-tocopherol recipients had more hemorrhagic stroke.
The CARET trial in smokers and asbestos-exposed workers also demonstrated harm from β-carotene (lung cancer 6 vs 5 per 1000/year).
These results contradict numerous observational studies and demonstrate the importance of testing chemoprevention hypotheses in randomized trials before widespread implementation.
keyPoints: β-carotene INCREASED lung cancer in smokers (ATBC and CARET)
α-tocopherol caused more hemorrhagic stroke in ATBC
Lesson: test chemoprevention in RCTs before adoption — observation can mislead
items.reference: CHEMOPREVENTION OF CANCERS OF THE UPPER AERODIGESTIVE TRACT: "The ATBC and CARET results demonstrate the importance of testing chemoprevention hypotheses in randomized trials before widespread implementation because the results contradict a number of observational studies."

items.title: How tamoxifen and raloxifene differ for breast-cancer prevention
items.content:
Selective estrogen-receptor modulators reduce breast-cancer risk with different harm profiles.
In a randomized prevention trial of high-risk women, tamoxifen decreased breast cancer risk by 49% and reduced fractures, but increased endometrial cancer, stroke, pulmonary embolism, and deep-vein thrombosis.
Raloxifene is comparable to tamoxifen in prevention but without the increased endometrial cancer risk and with fewer thromboembolic events (though a smaller reduction in invasive cancers).
Both are FDA-approved for risk reduction in high-risk women (≥1.66% 5-year risk by the Gail model; raloxifene for postmenopausal women only); aromatase inhibitors such as exemestane reduced invasive breast cancer by 65% in a trial.
keyPoints: Tamoxifen: ~49% risk reduction but ↑ endometrial cancer, stroke, VTE
Raloxifene: comparable prevention without endometrial cancer risk, fewer clots (postmenopausal only)
FDA-approved for ≥1.66% 5-year Gail-model risk; exemestane (AI) reduced invasive cancer 65%
items.reference: CHEMOPREVENTION OF BREAST CANCER: "tamoxifen decreased the risk of developing breast cancer by 49%...a small increase in risk of endometrial cancer, stroke, pulmonary emboli, and deep vein thrombosis was noted."

items.title: Why 5-alpha-reductase inhibitors are not approved for prostate-cancer prevention
items.content:
An apparent grade paradox limited adoption of a risk-reducing drug.
In the PCPT, finasteride reduced overall prostate cancer incidence (18.4% vs 24.4%), but the finasteride group had more high-grade (Gleason ≥7) tumors; dutasteride (REDUCE) similarly reduced incidence by 23% with a high-grade signal in years 3–4.
The higher-grade finding likely reflects increased sensitivity of PSA and digital rectal exam for high-grade tumors due to reduced prostate volume on 5-α-reductase inhibitors, though a causative role cannot be conclusively dismissed — so these agents are not FDA-approved for prostate cancer prevention.
Because all trial men were screened (which roughly doubles prostate cancer detection), it is unknown whether these drugs help unscreened men or just reduce non-life-threatening cancers.
keyPoints: Finasteride/dutasteride reduce prostate cancer incidence but with a high-grade tumor signal
Likely detection bias (smaller gland → more sensitive PSA/DRE), but causation not excluded
Not FDA-approved for prevention; screening roughly doubles detection
items.reference: CHEMOPREVENTION OF PROSTATE CANCER: "These agents are therefore not FDA-approved for prostate cancer prevention."

items.title: How vaccines prevent infection-associated cancers
items.content:
Several cancers are caused by infections that vaccines can block.
Hepatitis B and C are linked to liver cancer, some HPV strains to cervical/anal/oropharyngeal cancer, and Helicobacter pylori to gastric adenocarcinoma and lymphoma.
The hepatitis B vaccine is effective in preventing hepatitis and hepatomas due to chronic hepatitis B infection.
A nonavalent HPV vaccine (strains 6, 11, 16, 18, 31, 33, 45, 52, 58) prevents persistent infection and preneoplastic lesions in the not-previously-infected — reduction in HPV 16 and 18 alone could theoretically prevent >70% of cervical cancers worldwide — but it does not affect pre-existing infections.
keyPoints: HBV vaccine prevents hepatitis B-related hepatomas
Nonavalent HPV vaccine prevents persistent infection/preneoplasia; HPV 16/18 → >70% of cervical cancers
HPV vaccine does not treat existing infection (2-dose ages 9–14; 3-dose 15–26)
items.reference: VACCINES AND CANCER PREVENTION: "reduction in HPV types 16 and 18 alone could theoretically prevent >70% of cervical cancers worldwide."

items.title: Why cause-specific mortality — not survival — is the right screening endpoint
items.content:
Screening can look beneficial through biased metrics even when it saves no lives.
Screening detects disease in asymptomatic people to reduce morbidity and mortality, but early detection does not in itself confer benefit, and several biases can suggest benefit where there is none or even mask net harm.
For this reason, cause-specific mortality, rather than survival after diagnosis, is the preferred endpoint.
Because screening is done on healthy persons, it should offer a substantial likelihood of benefit that outweighs harm, and tests must be carefully evaluated before widespread use.
keyPoints: Early detection alone is not benefit; biases can simulate benefit or hide harm
Cause-specific mortality is the preferred endpoint, not survival-from-diagnosis
Screening healthy people must have benefit clearly outweighing harm
items.reference: CANCER SCREENING: "Cause-specific mortality, rather than survival after diagnosis, is the preferred endpoint."

items.title: Why predictive value (unlike sensitivity/specificity) depends on disease prevalence
items.content:
Test accuracy indices behave differently with disease frequency.
Sensitivity (true-positive rate) and specificity (1 minus false-positive rate) are relatively independent of the underlying prevalence of disease in the screened population.
In contrast, the positive and negative predictive values depend strongly on prevalence.
Screening is most beneficial when the target disease is common, and specificity is at least as important as sensitivity — for low-prevalence diseases like cancer, poor specificity dramatically lowers PPV so that only a small fraction of positive tests are true positives.
keyPoints: Sensitivity/specificity are ~prevalence-independent; PPV/NPV depend strongly on prevalence
Specificity is at least as important as sensitivity for feasibility
Low prevalence + poor specificity → low PPV (most positives are false positives)
items.reference: The Accuracy of Screening: "The sensitivity and specificity of a test are relatively independent of the underlying prevalence (or risk) of the disease in the population screened, but the predictive values depend strongly on the prevalence of the disease."

items.title: How lead-time and length-time bias make screening look better than it is
items.content:
Three biases systematically flatter screening tests.
Lead-time bias occurs whether or not a test influences the natural history: the patient is merely diagnosed earlier, so survival appears increased even if life is not prolonged.
Length-biased sampling occurs because screening more easily detects slow-growing, less aggressive cancers; its extreme form is overdiagnosis — detection of 'pseudodisease' that meets histologic criteria for cancer but would never become clinically significant.
Selection bias arises because screening volunteers tend to be more health-conscious with better prognosis (the healthy-volunteer effect).
keyPoints: Lead-time bias: earlier diagnosis lengthens apparent survival without prolonging life
Length bias/overdiagnosis: indolent 'pseudodisease' is preferentially detected
Selection bias: healthy-volunteer effect favors screened groups
items.reference: Potential Biases of Screening Tests: "Lead-time bias occurs whether or not a test influences the natural history of the disease; the patient is merely diagnosed at an earlier date. Survival appears increased even if life is not prolonged."

items.title: Why a randomized trial with cause-specific mortality is the strongest screening evidence
items.content:
Only a randomized design with the right endpoint reliably separates benefit from bias.
A randomized controlled screening trial with cause-specific mortality as the endpoint provides the strongest support for a screening intervention, and overall mortality should also be reported to detect adverse effects of screening and treatment on other outcomes.
Efficacy is established when the screened group has a lower cause-specific mortality rate than the control group.
Studies showing reduced advanced-stage incidence, improved survival, or a stage shift are weaker (and possibly misleading) evidence of benefit.
keyPoints: RCT with cause-specific mortality endpoint = strongest evidence; also report overall mortality
Benefit = lower cause-specific mortality in the screened arm
Stage shift / improved survival / less advanced-stage disease are weaker, possibly misleading
items.reference: Assessment of Screening Tests: "A randomized controlled screening trial with cause-specific mortality as the endpoint provides the strongest support for a screening intervention."

items.title: Why mammography screening for women 40–49 is controversial
items.content:
Benefit is clearer after 50 and contested in the 40s, where harms loom larger.
Trials suggest annual or biennial mammography in normal-risk women over 50 decreases breast cancer mortality by 15–30%, but experts disagree about ages 40–49.
The U.K. Age Trial found no statistically significant mortality difference for women 40–49, and a meta-analysis showed only an 8% relative reduction (≈3 deaths prevented per 10,000 women over 10 years, not statistically significant).
Meanwhile nearly half of women 40–49 screened annually will have a false-positive mammogram, and overdiagnosis is estimated at 10–50% of diagnosed invasive cancers; breast self-examination has not been shown to decrease mortality.
keyPoints: Mammography >50: ~15–30% breast cancer mortality reduction
Ages 40–49: benefit small/uncertain (~8% RR), with ~50% cumulative false-positive rate annually
Overdiagnosis 10–50% of invasive cancers; breast self-exam does not reduce mortality
items.reference: BREAST CANCER (Screening): "nearly half of women age 40–49 years screened annually will have false-positive mammograms necessitating further evaluation, often including biopsy. Estimates of overdiagnosis range from 10 to 50% of diagnosed invasive cancers."

items.title: How cervical cancer screening is structured by age and HPV testing
items.content:
Pap-based screening has sharply reduced cervical cancer mortality and is timed to HPV biology.
Screening recommends regular Pap testing for all women from age 21 (before this age, even with sexual activity, screening may cause more harm than benefit), at a recommended interval of 3 years.
From age 30, guidelines add HPV testing with or without Pap, and a normal co-test result may lengthen the interval to 5 years; screening more frequently adds little benefit but causes harms (unnecessary procedures, overtreatment of transient lesions).
Women aged 65 with no abnormal results in the previous 10 years may stop, and screening is discontinued after hysterectomy with cervical excision for noncancerous reasons.
keyPoints: Start at 21; Pap every 3 years (earlier screening causes net harm)
From 30: add HPV testing; normal co-test → interval up to 5 years
Stop at 65 if normal for 10 years; stop after hysterectomy with cervix removed for benign reasons
items.reference: CERVICAL CANCER: "Screening guidelines recommend regular Pap testing for all women who have reached the age of 21...The recommended interval for Pap screening is 3 years."

items.title: How the colorectal screening modalities compare
items.content:
Several tests reduce colorectal cancer mortality, with trade-offs in sensitivity and harm.
Digital rectal examination is not an effective screening test and is not recommended; FOBT reduces colorectal cancer mortality (~22% biennial, ~32% annual in one trial), but only 2–10% of those with occult blood actually have cancer, so its high false-positive rate drives many follow-up colonoscopies — FITs have higher sensitivity and have largely replaced FOBT.
Sigmoidoscopy reduces colorectal cancer incidence (~18%) and mortality (~28%); one-time colonoscopy detects about 25% more advanced lesions than FOBT-with-sigmoidoscopy.
Perforation rates are about 4/10,000 for colonoscopy versus 1/10,000 for sigmoidoscopy.
keyPoints: DRE is not effective and not recommended; FOBT cuts mortality (~22% biennial/~32% annual) but is non-specific
FIT > FOBT for sensitivity; sigmoidoscopy reduces incidence ~18% and mortality ~28%
Colonoscopy detects ~25% more advanced lesions; perforation ~4/10,000 (vs 1/10,000 for sigmoidoscopy)
items.reference: COLORECTAL CANCER: "A meta-analysis of five randomized controlled trials demonstrated a 22% relative reduction in colorectal cancer mortality after two to nine rounds of biennial FOBT...However, only 2–10% of those with occult blood in the stool actually have cancer."

items.title: Why low-dose CT (not chest x-ray) is used for lung screening, and why ovarian screening is not done
items.content:
Trial endpoints separate which screens help and which harm.
For lung cancer, annual chest x-ray did not reduce lung cancer death (PLCO), whereas low-dose CT in high-risk smokers (NLST: ages 55–74, 30+ pack-years) produced a statistically significant reduction of about 3 fewer deaths per 1000 people screened, at the cost of false positives, incidental findings, and radiation.
For ovarian cancer, a large randomized trial of annual transvaginal ultrasound plus CA-125 did not reduce ovarian cancer deaths, and the high false-positive rate led to many unnecessary surgeries (surgery-to-cancer ratio ~20:1).
In 2016 the FDA recommended against using any ovarian cancer screening test.
keyPoints: Lung: chest x-ray useless (PLCO); low-dose CT helps high-risk smokers (NLST, ~3 fewer deaths/1000)
Ovarian: TVUS + CA-125 does not reduce deaths; ~20:1 surgery-to-cancer ratio
FDA recommends against ovarian cancer screening tests
items.reference: LUNG CANCER: "It demonstrated a statistically significant reduction of about 3 fewer deaths per 1000 people screened with CT compared to chest x-ray after 12 years."

items.title: Why PSA screening's mortality benefit is at best small
items.content:
Two landmark trials bracket the modest, contested benefit of PSA screening.
PSA screening is prone to lead-time bias, length bias, and overdiagnosis, and men over 50 have a high prevalence (about 30–50%) of clinically insignificant prostate cancers that screening detects.
The U.S. PLCO trial found no statistically significant difference in prostate cancer deaths between PSA screening and usual care (though >50% of controls had a PSA test), while the European ERSPC trial found a 20% relative reduction in prostate cancer death in its core group, with 570 men needing to be invited to screening (18 cancers detected) to avert one death.
Screening must be linked to effective therapy to have any benefit, and treatments for low-stage disease can cause impotence and incontinence.
keyPoints: Effect of PSA screening on mortality is at best small (PLCO null; ERSPC 20% RR reduction)
ERSPC: ~570 invited / 18 cancers to avert 1 death; 30–50% of older men harbor insignificant cancers
Benefit requires effective therapy; treatment causes impotence/incontinence — counsel about uncertainty
items.reference: PROSTATE CANCER: "Randomized trials indicate that the effect of PSA screening on prostate cancer mortality across a population is, at best, small."

items.question: A 55-year-old quits a 35-pack-year smoking habit. By how much is his 10-year lung cancer mortality reduced compared with continuing to smoke?
items.explanation: Those who stop smoking have a 30–50% lower 10-year lung cancer mortality rate than continuing smokers, despite the persistence of some carcinogen-induced mutations.
items.reference: SMOKING CESSATION: "Those who stop smoking have a 30–50% lower 10-year lung cancer mortality rate compared to those who continue smoking..."

items.question: Why is tobacco-use prevention considered primarily a pediatric issue?
options: Children are more susceptible to nicotine toxicity
More than 80% of adult smokers began smoking before age 18
Cigarettes are marketed only to children
items.explanation: More than 80% of adult American smokers began before age 18, making prevention and counseling in adolescents and young adults critical.
items.reference: SMOKING CESSATION: "More than 80% of adult American smokers began smoking before the age of 18 years."

items.question: The Women's Health Initiative dietary intervention (low-fat, high fruit/vegetable/fiber) demonstrated which result for breast cancer over 8 years?
options: A 49% reduction in invasive breast cancer
No reduction in invasive breast cancer
An increase in invasive breast cancer
A reduction only in colorectal cancer
items.explanation: Despite lower dietary fat intake in the intervention group, invasive breast cancers were not reduced over 8 years, and there was no reduction in colorectal cancer either.
items.reference: DIET MODIFICATION: "invasive breast cancers were not reduced over an 8-year follow-up period compared to the control group."

items.question: What did the ATBC and CARET trials reveal about β-carotene supplementation in smokers?
options: It had no effect on lung cancer
items.explanation: Both ATBC and CARET demonstrated harm: β-carotene increased lung cancer in smokers, contradicting observational studies and underscoring the need for randomized trials before adopting chemoprevention.
items.reference: CHEMOPREVENTION OF CANCERS OF THE UPPER AERODIGESTIVE TRACT: "This trial also demonstrated harm from β-carotene..."

items.question: Compared with tamoxifen for breast cancer prevention, raloxifene offers which advantage?
options: Greater reduction in invasive breast cancer
No increased risk of endometrial cancer and fewer thromboembolic events
items.explanation: Raloxifene is comparable to tamoxifen in prevention but without the increased endometrial cancer risk and with fewer thromboembolic events (though a smaller reduction in invasive cancers); it is approved for postmenopausal women only.
items.reference: CHEMOPREVENTION OF BREAST CANCER: "raloxifene is comparable to tamoxifen in cancer prevention but without the risk of endometrial cancer...fewer thromboembolic events than tamoxifen."

items.question: Why are finasteride and dutasteride not FDA-approved for prostate cancer prevention despite reducing overall incidence?
options: They were associated with more high-grade (Gleason ≥7) tumors, raising concern not conclusively excluded as causal
They had no effect on incidence
items.explanation: Both trials showed reduced overall incidence but more high-grade tumors. This likely reflects detection bias (smaller gland → more sensitive PSA/DRE), but a causative role could not be conclusively dismissed, so they are not approved for prevention.
items.reference: CHEMOPREVENTION OF PROSTATE CANCER: "These agents are therefore not FDA-approved for prostate cancer prevention."

items.question: Reduction of which two HPV types alone could theoretically prevent more than 70% of cervical cancers worldwide?
items.explanation: HPV types 16 and 18 account for the majority of cervical cancers; reducing them alone could theoretically prevent >70% of cervical cancers worldwide. Types 6 and 11 cause genital papillomas.
items.reference: VACCINES AND CANCER PREVENTION: "reduction in HPV types 16 and 18 alone could theoretically prevent >70% of cervical cancers worldwide."

items.question: Which is the preferred endpoint for establishing the benefit of a cancer screening test?
items.explanation: Cause-specific mortality, rather than survival after diagnosis, is the preferred endpoint, because survival and stage shift are vulnerable to lead-time, length, and overdiagnosis biases.
items.reference: CANCER SCREENING: "Cause-specific mortality, rather than survival after diagnosis, is the preferred endpoint."

items.question: Which test characteristic depends strongly on the prevalence of disease in the screened population?
items.explanation: Sensitivity and specificity are relatively prevalence-independent, but predictive values (PPV and NPV) depend strongly on prevalence; for low-prevalence diseases like cancer, poor specificity sharply lowers PPV.
items.reference: The Accuracy of Screening: "the predictive values depend strongly on the prevalence of the disease."

items.question: A screening test diagnoses a cancer earlier but does not change when the patient dies; survival from diagnosis nonetheless appears longer. This is an example of:
items.explanation: Lead-time bias occurs when earlier diagnosis lengthens apparent survival without prolonging life — the patient is simply aware of the disease for longer.
items.reference: Potential Biases of Screening Tests: "the patient is merely diagnosed at an earlier date. Survival appears increased even if life is not prolonged."

items.question: The preferential detection by screening of indolent 'pseudodisease' that would never have caused symptoms is termed:
items.explanation: Overdiagnosis is an extreme form of length-biased sampling — detection of pseudodisease meeting histologic criteria for cancer that would never become clinically significant, leading to overtreatment.
items.reference: Potential Biases of Screening Tests: "An extreme form of length bias sampling is termed overdiagnosis, the detection of \"pseudo disease.\""

items.question: Which study design provides the strongest evidence to support a cancer screening test?
options: A case series showing earlier-stage diagnoses
An observational cohort showing improved survival
A randomized controlled trial with cause-specific mortality as the endpoint
items.explanation: A randomized controlled screening trial with cause-specific mortality as the endpoint provides the strongest support; stage shift and survival are weaker, potentially misleading endpoints.
items.reference: Assessment of Screening Tests: "A randomized controlled screening trial with cause-specific mortality as the endpoint provides the strongest support for a screening intervention."

items.question: For average-risk women older than 50, mammographic screening reduces breast cancer mortality by approximately:
items.explanation: Trials suggest annual or biennial mammography in normal-risk women over 50 decreases breast cancer mortality, in most studies by 15–30%. Benefit in women 40–49 is much smaller and contested.
items.reference: BREAST CANCER (Screening): "In most, breast cancer-related mortality rates were decreased by 15–30%."

items.question: What does the evidence show about breast self-examination as a screening method?
options: It reduces breast cancer mortality by 20%
It has not been shown to decrease mortality and increases benign biopsies
It is recommended for all women over 40
items.explanation: No study of breast self-examination has shown a mortality benefit; a large randomized trial in China found no mortality difference but more benign lesions and biopsies in the self-exam arm.
items.reference: BREAST CANCER (Screening): "No study of breast self-examination has shown it to decrease mortality."

items.question: At what age do guidelines recommend beginning cervical cancer screening with Pap testing?
options: At the onset of sexual activity regardless of age
items.explanation: Pap testing is recommended from age 21; before this age, even with sexual activity, screening may cause more harm than benefit. From age 30, HPV testing is added and the interval may extend to 5 years.
items.reference: CERVICAL CANCER: "Screening guidelines recommend regular Pap testing for all women who have reached the age of 21..."

items.question: Which statement about colorectal cancer screening modalities is correct?
options: Digital rectal examination is an effective recommended screening test
FIT has lower sensitivity than FOBT
Sigmoidoscopy reduces colorectal cancer incidence and mortality
Colonoscopy has a lower perforation rate than sigmoidoscopy
items.explanation: Sigmoidoscopy reduces colorectal cancer incidence (~18%) and mortality (~28%). DRE is not effective and not recommended; FIT has higher sensitivity than FOBT; and colonoscopy has a higher perforation rate (~4/10,000) than sigmoidoscopy (~1/10,000).
items.reference: COLORECTAL CANCER: "Two meta-analyses of five randomized controlled trials of sigmoidoscopy found an 18% relative reduction in colorectal cancer incidence and a 28% relative reduction in colorectal cancer mortality."

items.question: Which lung cancer screening approach reduced lung cancer mortality in a high-risk population (age 55–74, 30+ pack-years)?
items.explanation: In the NLST, low-dose CT produced a statistically significant reduction of about 3 fewer lung cancer deaths per 1000 people screened versus chest x-ray; annual chest x-ray (PLCO) did not reduce lung cancer death.
items.reference: LUNG CANCER: "the National Lung Screening Trial (NLST)...demonstrated a statistically significant reduction of about 3 fewer deaths per 1000 people screened with CT compared to chest x-ray..."

items.question: What is the current recommendation regarding ovarian cancer screening with transvaginal ultrasound and CA-125 in average-risk women?
options: It substantially reduces ovarian cancer mortality and is recommended
It does not reduce ovarian cancer deaths and is recommended against (FDA 2016)
It is recommended only over age 65
It is the preferred screen for BRCA carriers
items.explanation: A large randomized trial showed annual TVUS plus CA-125 did not reduce ovarian cancer deaths, with a high false-positive rate and ~20:1 surgery-to-cancer ratio; in 2016 the FDA recommended against any ovarian cancer screening test.
items.reference: OVARIAN CANCER: "the FDA issued a safety communication recommending against using any ovarian cancer screening test..."

items.question: In the European ERSPC trial of PSA screening, approximately how many men needed to be invited to screening to avert one prostate cancer death?
items.explanation: ERSPC found a 20% relative reduction in prostate cancer death in its core group, with 570 men needing to be invited to screening (18 cancers detected) to avert one death; the U.S. PLCO trial found no significant difference.
items.reference: PROSTATE CANCER: "570 men...would need to be invited to screening, and 18 cases of prostate cancer detected, to avert 1 death from prostate cancer."

items.question: The observation that most high-grade serous 'ovarian' cancers actually arise in which structure raises the possibility of prevention by ovary-sparing surgery?
items.explanation: Most high-grade serous ovarian cancers arise in the fallopian tube fimbria, raising the possibility that ovary-sparing salpingectomy could prevent this lethal subtype while avoiding the cardiovascular harms of premenopausal oophorectomy.
items.reference: SURGICAL PREVENTION OF CANCER: "most high-grade serous \"ovarian cancers\" actually arise in the fallopian tube fimbria raises the possibility that this lethal subtype may be prevented by ovary-sparing salpingectomy."

items.statement: Tobacco use causes more deaths from cardiovascular disease than from cancer.
items.explanation: True — although strongly carcinogenic, tobacco causes more deaths from cardiovascular disease than from cancer.
items.reference: SMOKING CESSATION: "Tobacco use causes more deaths from cardiovascular disease than from cancer."

items.statement: Light and low-tar cigarettes are a safer alternative for smokers who cannot quit.
items.explanation: False — light- and low-tar cigarettes are not safer because smokers tend to inhale them more frequently and deeply.
items.reference: SMOKING CESSATION: "Light- and low-tar cigarettes are not safer because smokers tend to inhale them more frequently and deeply."

items.statement: Physical activity is associated with a decreased risk of colon and breast cancer.
items.explanation: True — physical activity is associated with decreased risk of colon and breast cancer, though such studies are prone to confounding and reverse causality.
items.reference: PHYSICAL ACTIVITY: "Physical activity is associated with a decreased risk of colon and breast cancer."

items.statement: Vitamin, mineral, or nutritional supplements above the amounts in a balanced diet have proven anticarcinogenic value.
items.explanation: False — evidence does not currently establish the anticarcinogenic value of supplements in amounts greater than those provided by a balanced diet.
items.reference: DIET MODIFICATION: "Evidence does not currently establish the anticarcinogenic value of vitamin, mineral, or nutritional supplements in amounts greater than those provided by a balanced diet."

items.statement: About 70–80% of melanomas arise de novo rather than from existing benign nevi.
items.explanation: True — although a large number of benign or atypical nevi is a risk factor, about 70–80% of melanomas arise de novo rather than from pre-existing benign nevi.
items.reference: SUN AVOIDANCE: "about 70–80% of melanomas arise de novo, rather than from existing benign nevi."

items.statement: There is currently a chemopreventive agent with sufficient evidence to recommend for preventing colon cancer in the general population.
items.explanation: False — no agent with sufficient evidence exists at present for the prevention of colon cancer in the general population; aspirin/NSAID evidence is insufficient and carries bleeding harms, and COX-2 inhibitors increase cardiovascular events.
items.reference: CHEMOPREVENTION OF COLON CANCER: "No agent with sufficient evidence exists at present for the prevention of colon cancer in the general population."

items.statement: Sensitivity and specificity are relatively independent of the prevalence of disease in the population screened.
items.explanation: True — sensitivity and specificity are relatively prevalence-independent, whereas predictive values (PPV, NPV) depend strongly on prevalence.
items.reference: The Accuracy of Screening: "The sensitivity and specificity of a test are relatively independent of the underlying prevalence...but the predictive values depend strongly on the prevalence of the disease."

items.statement: A reduction in advanced-stage incidence or a stage shift definitively establishes the value of a screening test.
items.explanation: False — studies showing reduced advanced-stage disease, improved survival, or a stage shift are weaker (and possibly misleading) evidence; cause-specific mortality in a randomized trial is the strongest endpoint.
items.reference: Assessment of Screening Tests: "Studies showing a reduction in the incidence of advanced-stage disease, improved survival, or a stage shift are weaker (and possibly misleading) evidence of benefit."

items.statement: Only about 2–10% of people with a positive fecal occult blood test actually have colorectal cancer.
items.explanation: True — only 2–10% of those with occult blood in the stool actually have cancer, so FOBT's high false-positive rate drives many follow-up colonoscopies.
items.reference: COLORECTAL CANCER: "only 2–10% of those with occult blood in the stool actually have cancer."

items.statement: Annual chest x-ray screening reduces lung cancer mortality.
items.explanation: False — in the PLCO trial, annual chest x-ray did not reduce lung cancer death; low-dose CT, however, reduced mortality in high-risk smokers in the NLST.
items.reference: LUNG CANCER: "annual chest x-ray did not reduce the risk of dying from lung cancer (relative risk 0.99; 95% confidence interval, 0.87–1.22) after 13 years."

items.statement: Men older than 50 have a high prevalence (about 30–50%) of clinically insignificant prostate cancers.
items.explanation: True — about 30–50% of men over 50 harbor clinically insignificant prostate cancers (increasing with age), which contributes to overdiagnosis with PSA screening.
items.reference: PROSTATE CANCER: "men older than age 50 years have a high prevalence of clinically insignificant prostate cancers (about 30–50% of men, increasing further as men age)."

items.statement: Multicancer early detection (MCED) blood tests have proven health-outcome benefits when used to screen asymptomatic people.
items.explanation: False — most evidence comes from people already diagnosed with cancer, and no data are available on health outcomes from using MCED tests for screening asymptomatic people.
items.reference: MULTICANCER EARLY DETECTION TESTS: "no data are available on health outcomes associated with using MCED tests for cancer screening."

items.assertion: Chemoprevention hypotheses should be tested in randomized trials before widespread implementation.
items.reason: β-carotene, which observational studies suggested might protect against lung cancer, instead increased lung cancer in smokers in randomized trials.
items.explanation: Both are true and the reason explains the assertion: the ATBC/CARET reversal (harm from β-carotene) shows that observation can mislead, so randomized testing is essential before adoption.
items.reference: CHEMOPREVENTION OF CANCERS OF THE UPPER AERODIGESTIVE TRACT: "the results contradict a number of observational studies."

items.assertion: Improved survival after diagnosis in a screened group proves that the screening test saves lives.
items.reason: Cause-specific mortality is the preferred endpoint for evaluating a screening test.
items.explanation: The assertion is false — survival from diagnosis is inflated by lead-time and length bias and does not prove benefit. The reason is true (cause-specific mortality is preferred) and explains why the assertion is invalid. False assertion + true reason = option 3.
items.reference: CANCER SCREENING: "Cause-specific mortality, rather than survival after diagnosis, is the preferred endpoint."

items.assertion: Specificity is at least as important as sensitivity for the feasibility of a cancer screening test.
items.reason: For low-prevalence diseases such as cancer, poor specificity dramatically lowers the positive predictive value so that only a small fraction of positives are true positives.
items.explanation: Both are true and the reason explains the assertion: because cancer is uncommon, low specificity floods the program with false positives (low PPV), making specificity critical to feasibility.
items.reference: The Accuracy of Screening: "Specificity is at least as important to the ultimate feasibility and success of a screening test as sensitivity."

items.assertion: Smokers should be advised to quit completely rather than gradually cut down.
items.reason: Smokers who quit completely are more likely to succeed than those who gradually reduce or switch to lower-tar cigarettes.
items.explanation: Both are true and the reason explains the assertion: complete cessation has higher success rates, so it is the recommended approach.
items.reference: SMOKING CESSATION: "Smokers who quit completely are more likely to be successful than those who gradually reduce the number of cigarettes smoked..."

items.assertion: The HPV vaccine should ideally be given before the onset of sexual activity.
items.reason: The vaccine prevents persistent strain-specific infection in those not previously infected but does not affect pre-existing infections.
items.explanation: Both are true and the reason explains the assertion: because the vaccine cannot clear established infection, vaccinating before exposure maximizes benefit.
items.reference: VACCINES AND CANCER PREVENTION: "The vaccine does not appear to impact preexisting infections."

items.assertion: Cervical cancer screening should not begin before age 21 even in sexually active adolescents.
items.reason: Before age 21, screening may cause more harm than benefit.
items.explanation: Both are true and the reason explains the assertion: because earlier screening yields net harm (overtreatment of transient lesions), it is deferred to age 21.
items.reference: CERVICAL CANCER: "before this age, even in individuals that have begun sexual activity, screening may cause more harm than benefit."

items.assertion: Routine annual mammography in women age 40–49 is universally recommended because its mortality benefit clearly outweighs harm.
items.reason: Nearly half of women age 40–49 screened annually will have a false-positive mammogram requiring further evaluation.
items.explanation: The assertion is false — benefit in 40–49 is small/uncertain and experts disagree. The reason is true (high cumulative false-positive rate) and is one reason the benefit-harm balance is contested. False assertion + true reason = option 3.
items.reference: BREAST CANCER (Screening): "nearly half of women age 40–49 years screened annually will have false-positive mammograms necessitating further evaluation..."

items.assertion: PSA screening must be linked to effective therapy to provide any mortality benefit.
items.reason: Detecting prostate cancer earlier cannot reduce deaths unless effective treatment changes the disease course.
items.explanation: Both are true and the reason explains the assertion: screening only helps if it leads to treatment that alters outcomes; without effective therapy, earlier detection alone does not save lives.
items.reference: PROSTATE CANCER: "Screening must be linked to effective therapy to have any benefit."

items.assertion: Observational studies of prophylactic mastectomy and salpingo-oophorectomy may overestimate the benefit of these procedures.
items.reason: Such studies are prone to biases including case-selection bias and family relationships between patients and controls.
items.explanation: Both are true and the reason explains the assertion: because all the evidence is observational and bias-prone, the magnitude of benefit may be overestimated.
items.reference: SURGICAL PREVENTION OF CANCER: "such studies are prone to a variety of biases...Thus, they may give an overestimate of the magnitude of benefit."

items.assertion: Routine ovarian cancer screening with CA-125 and transvaginal ultrasound is recommended for average-risk women.
items.reason: These tests have a high false-positive rate that leads to many unnecessary surgeries.
items.explanation: The assertion is false — these tests do not reduce ovarian cancer deaths and the FDA recommends against them. The reason is true (high false positives, ~20:1 surgery-to-cancer ratio) and explains why screening is not recommended. False assertion + true reason = option 3.
items.reference: OVARIAN CANCER: "The risks and costs associated with the high number of false-positive results are impediments to routine use of these modalities for screening."
